---
title: "The Engine"
description: "Act III of a beginner-friendly journey toward Borwein integrals."
date: 2026-06-04
categories: [mathematics, integrals, visualization]
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: true
    code-tools: true
execute:
  echo: false
---

## What Act II left unexplained

In Act II, we found the plateau:

$$
\int_0^\infty
S_{a_0}(x)S_{a_1}(x)\cdots S_{a_n}(x)\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2a_0}
$$

whenever

$$
a_0\geq a_1+a_2+\cdots+a_n.
$$

Here

$$
S_a(x)=\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}.
$$

The random-walk explanation was simple: the smaller steps all fit inside the largest safe window.

But the story is larger.

What if we multiply by cosine factors?

What if the target is not zero?

What if we replace

$$
\frac{\sin(ax)}{ax}
$$

by the cosine-difference cousin

$$
\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{(ax)^2}?
$$

What if we go further and use

$$
\frac{ax-\sin(ax)}{x^3},
\qquad
\frac{\cos(ax)-1+\frac{a^2x^2}{2}}{x^4},
$$

and the higher Taylor-remainder families?

This act opens the engine under the hood.

```{ojs}
Plot = require("@observablehq/plot@0.6")

sineFrac = (x, a) => Math.abs(a * x) < 1e-8 ? 1 : Math.sin(a * x) / (a * x)

cosDiffHalf = (x, a) => {
  const z = a * x;
  return Math.abs(z) < 1e-7 ? 0.5 : (1 - Math.cos(z)) / (z * z);
}

cosDiffUnit = (x, a) => 2 * cosDiffHalf(x, a)

fact = n => {
  let out = 1;
  for (let k = 2; k <= n; k++) out *= k;
  return out;
}

binom = (n, k) => {
  if (k < 0 || k > n) return 0;
  return fact(n) / (fact(k) * fact(n - k));
}

prod = arr => arr.reduce((p, x) => p * x, 1)

sgn = x => x > 1e-11 ? 1 : (x < -1e-11 ? -1 : 0)

fmt = x => Number.isFinite(x) ? x.toFixed(12) : "not finite"
fmtShort = x => Number.isFinite(x) ? x.toFixed(8) : "not finite"

trapIntegral = (f, R, N = 9000) => {
  const h = R / N;
  let total = 0.5 * (f(0) + f(R));
  for (let i = 1; i < N; i++) total += f(i * h);
  return total * h;
}

normalizedSineCosineExact = (a0, extras, cosines) => {
  const n = extras.length;
  const m = cosines.length;
  const total = n + m;
  const masks = Math.pow(2, total);
  let signSum = 0;

  for (let mask = 0; mask < masks; mask++) {
    let b = a0;
    let eps = 1;

    for (let i = 0; i < n; i++) {
      const gamma = (mask & (1 << i)) ? 1 : -1;
      eps *= gamma;
      b += gamma * extras[i];
    }

    for (let j = 0; j < m; j++) {
      const eta = (mask & (1 << (n + j))) ? 1 : -1;
      b += eta * cosines[j];
    }

    signSum += eps * Math.pow(b, n) * sgn(b);
  }

  const denom = Math.pow(2, n + m) * fact(n) * a0 * prod(extras);
  return Math.PI * signSum / (2 * denom);
}

psi = (p, a, x) => {
  const z = a * x;
  if (Math.abs(z) < 1e-6) return 1;

  if (p === 0) return Math.sin(z) / z;
  if (p === 1) return 2 * (1 - Math.cos(z)) / (z * z);
  if (p === 2) return 6 * (z - Math.sin(z)) / Math.pow(z, 3);
  if (p === 3) return 24 * (Math.cos(z) - 1 + z * z / 2) / Math.pow(z, 4);
  if (p === 4) return 120 * (Math.sin(z) - z + Math.pow(z, 3) / 6) / Math.pow(z, 5);

  return NaN;
}

powerTentDensity = (t, p, a) => {
  if (Math.abs(t) > a) return 0;
  return (p + 1) * Math.pow(a - Math.abs(t), p) / (2 * Math.pow(a, p + 1));
}

twoPowerExact = (p, q, a, b) => {
  let sum = 0;

  if (a >= b) {
    for (let j = 0; j <= p; j++) {
      sum += binom(p, j) * Math.pow(a - b, p - j) * Math.pow(b, q + j + 1) / (q + j + 1);
    }
  } else {
    for (let j = 0; j <= q; j++) {
      sum += binom(q, j) * Math.pow(b - a, q - j) * Math.pow(a, p + j + 1) / (p + j + 1);
    }
  }

  return Math.PI * (p + 1) * (q + 1) * sum / (2 * Math.pow(a, p + 1) * Math.pow(b, q + 1));
}
```

## Cosine factors: moving the target

The first extension is to multiply by a cosine factor:

$$
\cos(cx).
$$

This gives an integral like

$$
\int_0^\infty
\cos(cx)
S_{a_0}(x)S_{a_1}(x)\cdots S_{a_n}(x)\,dx.
$$

The random-walk interpretation is friendly:

> Instead of asking whether the bug lands near $0$, we ask whether it lands near the target $c$.

The plateau condition becomes

$$
a_0\geq a_1+a_2+\cdots+a_n+|c|.
$$

If that holds, then

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty
\cos(cx)
S_{a_0}(x)S_{a_1}(x)\cdots S_{a_n}(x)\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2a_0}.
}
$$

So the cosine does not change the height of the plateau. It moves the target, and moving the target makes the plateau easier to break.

```{ojs}
viewof a0_move = Inputs.range([0.5, 3], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "safe radius a0"
})

viewof a1_move = Inputs.range([0.05, 1.5], {
  value: 0.3,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "extra size a1"
})

viewof a2_move = Inputs.range([0.05, 1.5], {
  value: 0.2,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "extra size a2"
})

viewof c_move = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {
  value: 0.25,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "target c"
})
```

```{ojs}
moveSum = a1_move + a2_move + Math.abs(c_move)
moveSafe = a0_move >= moveSum
moveExact = normalizedSineCosineExact(a0_move, [a1_move, a2_move], [c_move])
movePlateau = Math.PI / (2 * a0_move)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.65;">
  Boundary budget: <b>a1 + a2 + |c| = ${fmtShort(moveSum)}</b><br>
  Plateau condition: <b>a0 ≥ a1 + a2 + |c|</b><br>
  Is the condition satisfied? <b>${moveSafe ? "yes" : "no"}</b><br>
  Exact value from the plus/minus engine: <b>${fmt(moveExact)}</b><br>
  Plateau height π/(2a0): <b>${fmt(movePlateau)}</b>
</div>
`
```

```{ojs}
moveData = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => {
  const x = i * 80 / 999;
  return {
    x: x,
    y: Math.cos(c_move * x) * sineFrac(x, a0_move) * sineFrac(x, a1_move) * sineFrac(x, a2_move)
  };
})

Plot.line(moveData, {
  x: "x",
  y: "y"
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "integrand"},
  grid: true
})
```

This is the moving-target version of the plateau.

---

## The plus/minus engine

The complete sine-fraction plus cosine formula looks intimidating, but the idea is simple.

You take all possible plus/minus choices of the parameters, add them up in a signed way, and the answer drops out.

Let

$$
I_{n,m}
=
\int_0^\infty
\left[
\prod_{k=0}^{n} S_{a_k}(x)
\right]
\left[
\prod_{j=1}^{m}\cos(c_jx)
\right]dx.
$$

For each choice of signs, define

$$
b
=
a_0
\pm a_1
\pm a_2
\pm\cdots
\pm a_n
\pm c_1
\pm\cdots
\pm c_m.
$$

The signs attached to $a_1,\ldots,a_n$ also create a multiplier

$$
\varepsilon=(\text{sign of }a_1)(\text{sign of }a_2)\cdots(\text{sign of }a_n).
$$

Then the closed form is

$$
\boxed{
I_{n,m}
=
\frac{\pi}
{2a_0a_1\cdots a_n}
\cdot
\frac{1}{2^{n+m}n!}
\sum
\varepsilon\, b^n\,\operatorname{sgn}(b).
}
$$

The symbol $\sum$ means: add this contribution over every possible plus/minus choice.

This is the engine under the hood.

You do not have to memorize it. The important part is the boundary condition it reveals:

$$
a_0\geq a_1+\cdots+a_n+|c_1|+\cdots+|c_m|.
$$

If that condition holds, the integral stays on the plateau:

$$
\boxed{
I_{n,m}=\frac{\pi}{2a_0}.
}
$$

---

## Cosine-difference factors are not strangers

Now return to the cosine-difference cousin from Act I:

$$
E_a(x)=\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{(ax)^2}.
$$

The unit-normalized version is

$$
T_a(x)=2E_a(x)
=
\frac{2(1-\cos(ax))}{(ax)^2}.
$$

The crucial identity is

$$
T_a(x)
=
\left[
\frac{\sin\left(\frac{a x}{2}\right)}
{\frac{a x}{2}}
\right]^2.
$$

In our notation,

$$
T_a(x)=S_{a/2}(x)^2.
$$

So a product involving $T_a(x)$ is just a product involving repeated normalized sine fractions at half scale.

For example,

$$
\int_0^\infty
S_{a_0}(x)T_\beta(x)\,dx
=
\int_0^\infty
S_{a_0}(x)S_{\beta/2}(x)S_{\beta/2}(x)\,dx.
$$

So the cosine-difference cousin is already inside the same engine.

```{ojs}
viewof a0_tent = Inputs.range([0.5, 4], {
  value: 1.5,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "a0"
})

viewof beta_tent = Inputs.range([0.1, 5], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "beta"
})

viewof c_tent = Inputs.range([-2, 2], {
  value: 0,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "target c"
})
```

```{ojs}
tentExact = normalizedSineCosineExact(a0_tent, [beta_tent / 2, beta_tent / 2], [c_tent])
tentApprox = trapIntegral(
  x => Math.cos(c_tent * x) * sineFrac(x, a0_tent) * cosDiffUnit(x, beta_tent),
  140,
  9000
)
tentSafe = a0_tent >= beta_tent + Math.abs(c_tent)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.65;">
  Because T_beta equals two half-scale sine fractions, the extra budget is beta.<br>
  Plateau condition: <b>a0 ≥ beta + |c|</b><br>
  Is the condition satisfied? <b>${tentSafe ? "yes" : "no"}</b><br>
  Exact value from the repeated-factor formula: <b>${fmt(tentExact)}</b><br>
  Numerical approximation over a large finite interval: <b>${fmt(tentApprox)}</b>
</div>
`
```

This is why products of normalized sine fractions with products of $T$-factors are not a separate problem. They are repeated-parameter versions of the same problem.

For the half-normalized cousin $E_a(x)$, just remember

$$
E_a(x)=\frac12T_a(x).
$$

So each $E$-factor contributes one extra factor of $1/2$.

---

## The next ladder: higher Taylor remainders

The normalized sine fraction and the cosine-difference cousin are only the first two rungs of a larger ladder.

Define

$$
R_p(a,x)
=
\int_0^a
\frac{(a-t)^p}{p!}\cos(tx)\,dt.
$$

The first few are

$$
R_0(a,x)=\frac{\sin(ax)}{x},
$$

$$
R_1(a,x)=\frac{1-\cos(ax)}{x^2},
$$

$$
R_2(a,x)=\frac{ax-\sin(ax)}{x^3},
$$

and

$$
R_3(a,x)=
\frac{\cos(ax)-1+\frac{a^2x^2}{2}}{x^4}.
$$

The magic property is

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial a}R_p(a,x)=R_{p-1}(a,x).
$$

So differentiating moves one rung down the ladder.

If we differentiate enough times, every higher Taylor-remainder kernel eventually becomes the sine-over-$x$ kernel.

This is why the derivative property is so powerful.

For comparisons, it is cleaner to normalize each kernel so it starts at $1$:

$$
\Psi_{p,a}(x)=\frac{R_p(a,x)}{R_p(a,0)}.
$$

Then

$$
\Psi_{p,a}(0)=1.
$$

The first few are

$$
\Psi_{0,a}(x)=S_a(x),
$$

$$
\Psi_{1,a}(x)=T_a(x),
$$

$$
\Psi_{2,a}(x)=
\frac{6(ax-\sin(ax))}{a^3x^3},
$$

and

$$
\Psi_{3,a}(x)=
\frac{24\left(\cos(ax)-1+\frac{a^2x^2}{2}\right)}{a^4x^4}.
$$

---

## What these higher kernels mean physically

The random-walk interpretation also continues.

The kernel $\Psi_{p,a}(x)$ is the wave-shadow of a random step supported on

$$
[-a,a].
$$

But the step is not always equally likely.

For $p=0$, every step in $[-a,a]$ is equally likely. The density is flat.

For $p=1$, the density is triangular. The bug is more likely to choose a step near $0$ than a step near the edge.

For $p=2$, the density is a rounded parabolic tent.

For larger $p$, the bug becomes more and more biased toward small steps.

```{ojs}
viewof p_shape = Inputs.range([0, 6], {
  value: 0,
  step: 1,
  label: "p"
})

viewof a_shape = Inputs.range([0.5, 5], {
  value: 2,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})
```

```{ojs}
densityData = Array.from({length: 800}, (_, i) => {
  const t = -a_shape + 2 * a_shape * i / 799;
  return {
    t: t,
    density: powerTentDensity(t, p_shape, a_shape)
  };
})

Plot.line(densityData, {
  x: "t",
  y: "density"
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "step value"},
  y: {label: "probability density"},
  grid: true
})
```

This picture is important.

The original plateau came from flatness: the largest window treated every interior landing point equally.

Higher kernels are smoother, but less flat. That changes the behavior of products.

---

## Product of one higher family

The logical next experiment is:

$$
\int_0^\infty
\Psi_{p,a}(x)\Psi_{p,b}(x)\,dx.
$$

Assume $a\geq b$.

Then the exact value is

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty
\Psi_{p,a}(x)\Psi_{p,b}(x)\,dx
=
\pi
\frac{(p+1)^2}{2a^{p+1}b^{p+1}}
\sum_{j=0}^{p}
\binom{p}{j}
(a-b)^{p-j}
\frac{b^{p+j+1}}{p+j+1}.
}
$$

For $p=0$, this becomes the old two-factor plateau:

$$
\int_0^\infty S_a(x)S_b(x)\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2a}.
$$

For $p=1$, however,

$$
\int_0^\infty T_a(x)T_b(x)\,dx
=
\pi\left(\frac1a-\frac{b}{3a^2}\right).
$$

This still depends on $b$.

So the perfect plateau is special to the flat case.

```{ojs}
viewof p_one = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 1,
  step: 1,
  label: "family order p"
})

viewof a_one = Inputs.range([0.5, 5], {
  value: 3,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})

viewof b_one = Inputs.range([0.1, 5], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "b"
})
```

```{ojs}
oneExact = twoPowerExact(p_one, p_one, a_one, b_one)
oneApprox = trapIntegral(
  x => psi(p_one, a_one, x) * psi(p_one, b_one, x),
  140,
  9000
)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.65;">
  Exact value: <b>${fmt(oneExact)}</b><br>
  Numerical approximation over a large finite interval: <b>${fmt(oneApprox)}</b><br>
  For p = 0, the smaller scale disappears when it is inside the larger one.<br>
  For p > 0, the answer usually still depends on both scales.
</div>
`
```

```{ojs}
oneData = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => {
  const x = i * 60 / 999;
  return {
    x: x,
    y: psi(p_one, a_one, x) * psi(p_one, b_one, x)
  };
})

Plot.line(oneData, {
  x: "x",
  y: "y"
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "product"},
  grid: true
})
```

---

## Product of two different higher families

Now try two different rungs:

$$
\int_0^\infty
\Psi_{p,a}(x)\Psi_{q,b}(x)\,dx.
$$

If $a\geq b$, then

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty
\Psi_{p,a}(x)\Psi_{q,b}(x)\,dx
=
\pi
\frac{(p+1)(q+1)}{2a^{p+1}b^{q+1}}
\sum_{j=0}^{p}
\binom{p}{j}
(a-b)^{p-j}
\frac{b^{q+j+1}}{q+j+1}.
}
$$

If $b\geq a$, swap the roles of the two families.

This single formula includes the mixed example from Act I:

$$
S_a(x)T_b(x).
$$

It also includes genuinely new-looking combinations like

$$
T_a(x)
\cdot
\frac{6(bx-\sin(bx))}{b^3x^3}.
$$

```{ojs}
viewof p_two = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 0,
  step: 1,
  label: "first family p"
})

viewof q_two = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 2,
  step: 1,
  label: "second family q"
})

viewof a_two = Inputs.range([0.5, 5], {
  value: 3,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a"
})

viewof b_two = Inputs.range([0.1, 5], {
  value: 1.5,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "b"
})
```

```{ojs}
twoExact = twoPowerExact(p_two, q_two, a_two, b_two)
twoApprox = trapIntegral(
  x => psi(p_two, a_two, x) * psi(q_two, b_two, x),
  140,
  9000
)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.65;">
  Exact value: <b>${fmt(twoExact)}</b><br>
  Numerical approximation over a large finite interval: <b>${fmt(twoApprox)}</b>
</div>
`
```

---

## Product of many families

The pattern does not stop at two.

Take any list of families:

$$
\Psi_{p_0,a_0}(x),
\Psi_{p_1,a_1}(x),
\ldots,
\Psi_{p_N,a_N}(x).
$$

Then

$$
\int_0^\infty
\Psi_{p_0,a_0}(x)
\Psi_{p_1,a_1}(x)
\cdots
\Psi_{p_N,a_N}(x)
\,dx
$$

has a random-walk meaning:

> It equals $\pi$ times the height at zero of the final-position density of a bug whose different steps may come from different Taylor-remainder families.

In compact form,

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty
\prod_{i=0}^{N}
\Psi_{p_i,a_i}(x)\,dx
=
\pi f_{X_0+\cdots+X_N}(0).
}
$$

Here $X_i$ is a random step supported on $[-a_i,a_i]$ with density proportional to

$$
(a_i-|t|)^{p_i}.
$$

This is the many-family version.

It is possible because wave-shadows multiply when independent random steps are added.

```{ojs}
viewof p0_many = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 0,
  step: 1,
  label: "p0"
})

viewof p1_many = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 1,
  step: 1,
  label: "p1"
})

viewof p2_many = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 2,
  step: 1,
  label: "p2"
})

viewof a0_many = Inputs.range([0.5, 4], {
  value: 2.5,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a0"
})

viewof a1_many = Inputs.range([0.5, 4], {
  value: 1.5,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a1"
})

viewof a2_many = Inputs.range([0.5, 4], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.1,
  label: "a2"
})
```

```{ojs}
manyApprox = trapIntegral(
  x => psi(p0_many, a0_many, x) * psi(p1_many, a1_many, x) * psi(p2_many, a2_many, x),
  160,
  11000
)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.65;">
  Numerical approximation for the three-family product:
  <b>${fmt(manyApprox)}</b><br>
  The exact general object is a piecewise-polynomial convolution density.
</div>
`
```

```{ojs}
manyData = Array.from({length: 1000}, (_, i) => {
  const x = i * 60 / 999;
  return {
    x: x,
    y: psi(p0_many, a0_many, x) * psi(p1_many, a1_many, x) * psi(p2_many, a2_many, x)
  };
})

Plot.line(manyData, {
  x: "x",
  y: "y"
}).plot({
  width: 760,
  height: 360,
  x: {label: "x"},
  y: {label: "product"},
  grid: true
})
```

---

## The derivative machine

There is also a symbolic way to understand why this works.

Recall

$$
\frac{\partial}{\partial a}R_p(a,x)=R_{p-1}(a,x).
$$

So, after differentiating $p$ times,

$$
\frac{\partial^p}{\partial a^p}R_p(a,x)=R_0(a,x)=\frac{\sin(ax)}{x}.
$$

Therefore, if we start with a product of higher Taylor-remainder kernels and differentiate each parameter enough times, we descend to a product of sine-over-$x$ factors.

That product is exactly the one handled by the Borwein plus/minus engine.

Then, in principle, we integrate back.

So the machine is:

$$
\boxed{
\text{differentiate down to sine factors}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{apply the plus/minus formula}
\quad\longrightarrow\quad
\text{integrate back}.
}
$$

This is why the many-family extension is not a guess. It is structurally forced by the derivative ladder.

---

## The soft-breach law

Now we reach the most interesting extension.

Suppose we keep one flat safe-window factor:

$$
S_{a_0}(x),
$$

but allow the other steps to come from higher families:

$$
\Psi_{p_1,a_1}(x),
\Psi_{p_2,a_2}(x),
\ldots,
\Psi_{p_N,a_N}(x).
$$

Consider

$$
\int_0^\infty
\cos(cx)
S_{a_0}(x)
\prod_{k=1}^{N}\Psi_{p_k,a_k}(x)
\,dx.
$$

The plateau condition is still

$$
a_0\geq a_1+\cdots+a_N+|c|.
$$

If it holds, then

$$
\boxed{
\int_0^\infty
\cos(cx)
S_{a_0}(x)
\prod_{k=1}^{N}\Psi_{p_k,a_k}(x)
\,dx
=
\frac{\pi}{2a_0}.
}
$$

The reason is still the same: every possible final position lies inside the safe window.

Now suppose the boundary is barely crossed on one side.

Let

$$
\delta=a_1+\cdots+a_N+c-a_0>0.
$$

If $\delta$ is small enough that only one extreme corner is lost, then the lost probability is

$$
\boxed{
\frac{\delta^{N+P}}
{(N+P)!}
\prod_{k=1}^{N}
\frac{(p_k+1)!}{2a_k^{p_k+1}},
\qquad
P=p_1+\cdots+p_N.
}
$$

So the integral becomes

$$
\boxed{
\frac{\pi}{2a_0}
\left[
1-
\frac{\delta^{N+P}}
{(N+P)!}
\prod_{k=1}^{N}
\frac{(p_k+1)!}{2a_k^{p_k+1}}
\right].
}
$$

This is the soft-breach law.

For flat steps, all $p_k=0$, and the defect is proportional to $\delta^N$.

For triangular steps, all $p_k=1$, and the defect is proportional to $\delta^{2N}$.

For parabolic-tent steps, all $p_k=2$, and the defect is proportional to $\delta^{3N}$.

The smoother the bug's step behavior near the edge, the softer the collision with the boundary.

```{ojs}
viewof softN = Inputs.range([1, 5], {
  value: 3,
  step: 1,
  label: "number of non-flat steps N"
})

viewof softP = Inputs.range([0, 4], {
  value: 1,
  step: 1,
  label: "common family order p"
})

viewof softDelta = Inputs.range([0.001, 0.4], {
  value: 0.08,
  step: 0.001,
  label: "small breach delta"
})

viewof softA = Inputs.range([0.5, 3], {
  value: 1,
  step: 0.05,
  label: "common step scale a"
})
```

```{ojs}
softTotalP = softN * softP
softExponent = softN + softTotalP
softLost = Math.pow(softDelta, softExponent) /
  fact(softExponent) *
  Math.pow(fact(softP + 1) / (2 * Math.pow(softA, softP + 1)), softN)

html`
<div style="font-size: 1.05rem; line-height: 1.65;">
  Defect exponent: <b>N + P = ${softExponent}</b><br>
  Lost probability in the first-corner regime:
  <b>${softLost.toExponential(6)}</b><br>
  Increasing p makes the first breach dramatically softer.
</div>
`
```

---

## The punchline

The famous plateau is not a trigonometric accident.

It is the shadow of a flat random walker trapped inside a safe window.

Cosine factors move the target.

Cosine-difference factors are repeated half-scale sine-fraction factors.

Higher Taylor remainders create smoother random walkers.

And the derivative ladder explains why all of these families belong to the same machine.

The journey began with

$$
\frac{\sin x}{x}.
$$

It ends with a much larger principle:

> These integrals measure how random-walk distributions collide with boundaries.

That is why the pattern holds.

That is why it breaks.

And that is why the way it breaks contains new mathematics.
